rank_note: Rank intentionally null. site/src/data/indexes/us-states.json contains only 21 of 51 jurisdictions; ranks 9-38 were lost in the original legacy-HTML extraction and the surviving entries were renumbered contiguously, so the displayed rank column is not a national rank. Hawaii is currently displayed at rank 1, which is the single most exposed unsupported claim in the index. It must not be reassigned a rank until the index is rebuilt with all 51 jurisdictions.
score_delta_note: The published composite of 95.9 is a bulk-import placeholder with last_assessed null. It was never produced by an assessor and has no evidentiary basis. The delta measures the correction of a data defect, not institutional decline. Roughly 10 points of the delta come from the integration premium alone: the placeholder assigned near-uniform 4.5 values across all eight dimensions, producing a standard deviation of 0.17 and zero dimensions below 4.0, which awarded the full 10-point premium. Under evidence every dimension falls below 4.0, the weakness factor drops to 0 and the premium is 0.

band_change.note: Band change from Exemplary to Functional reflects replacement of an unsupported placeholder with a first evidence-based baseline. It is not a finding that Hawaii became less compassionate.

calibration_note: Not calibrated to the published us-states rank order, which is known to be corrupt, and explicitly not anchored to the published 95.9 placeholder. Calibrated to the methodology anchors and to the 17 states assessed at full depth under this methodology: Georgia 26.9, Iowa 30.6, Arizona 36.2, Ohio 38.1, North Carolina 45.0, Alaska 47.5, Oregon 50.6, Colorado 52.5, Wisconsin 53.1, Michigan 53.8, Delaware 53.8, Pennsylvania 54.4, Virginia 55.6, Maryland 55.6, New York 56.9, Illinois 60.0, New Jersey 60.6. Hawaii at 56.3 sits between Maryland 55.6 and New York 56.9. Composite verified by running computeCompositeFromDimensions from site/scripts/lib/scoring.mjs (methodology v1.2). Verification output: {"composite":56.3,"band":"Functional","integrationPremium":0}. Inputs AWR 3.4, EMP 3.2, ACT 3.0, EQU 3.4, BND 2.4, ACC 3.8, SYS 3.8, INT 3.0. baseComposite 56.25; stdDev 0.44 so consistencyMult 1.0; 8 of 8 dimensions below 4.0 so weaknessFactor 0 and integrationPremium 0.0; final composite 56.3, band Functional. The composite sits 3.7 points below the 60 band boundary, so the band is stable and no instability flag is warranted. Three sensitivity cases were also run through computeCompositeFromDimensions rather than computed by hand: raising BND from 2.4 to 3.4 (if the Front Street scope-clarity, state hospital refusal and corrections workforce findings were all remediated) returns {"composite":59.4,"band":"Functional","integrationPremium":0}; subtracting 0.3 from every dimension returns {"composite":48.7,"band":"Functional","integrationPremium":0}; adding 0.5 to every dimension returns {"composite":68.8,"band":"Established","integrationPremium":0}. Hawaii therefore cannot reach the Established band on any plausible near-term correction of a single dimension. For reference, running the published placeholder dimensions through the same function returns {"composite":95.9,"band":"Exemplary","integrationPremium":10}, confirming the published 95.9 is an artifact of uniform placeholder values plus the full 10-point integration premium.
attribution_note: Scores State of Hawaii conduct only, across the executive and legislative branches, since the benchmark scores the jurisdiction rather than an administration. Hawaii was under unified Democratic control for the whole 2021-2026 window (Governor Ige through December 2022, Governor Green from December 2022), so no divided-government attribution caveat applies. County conduct is excluded: Maui County contributed $10 million to the settlement against the state’s $808 million, and Lahaina permitting throughput is county action, so rebuild counts are used as context for state responsiveness rather than scored as state failure alone. Federal policy imposed on Hawaii is excluded. The 2025 federal reconciliation law (H.R. 1), which the Healthcare Association of Hawaii estimates will cost roughly 40,000 residents their coverage, is not scored against the state; what is scored is the state’s own Prepaid Health Care Act of 1974 and Med-QUEST reach, which are state discretion. FEMA decision backlogs affecting roughly a thousand Lahaina families are federal and are cited only as context for E1; the state-controlled One ʻOhana Fund timeline is what carries the AC1 score. Restoration of federal Medicaid eligibility for COFA migrants in December 2020 was federal action and is not credited to the state; the 2019 US Commission on Civil Rights finding of discrimination against Micronesian residents is scored because it documents state agency conduct. The Saguaro Correctional Center contract in Eloy, Arizona is a discretionary state procurement decision and is scored in full under I4.
key_evidence: Hawaii is published at rank 1 with a composite of 95.9 and last_assessed null. That number was never produced by an assessor. The first evidence-based baseline is 56.3, band Functional.

Attorney General Anne Lopez commissioned an independent outside investigation of the state’s own response to the Lahaina fire and published all three phases, producing 84 findings and 140 recommendations by 2025-01-14.

The state committed $808 million of the $4.04 billion Maui settlement and $65 million to the One ʻOhana Fund before any court determined liability. As of 2026-06-07 the fund had paid 79 death and serious-injury claims totalling $111.5 million.
The first payment from the state’s own One ʻOhana Fund came on 2025-03-15, 19 months after the August 2023 fire.
As of July 2026, 234 structures were completed in the Lahaina burn area against roughly 2,200 destroyed, nearly three years after the fire.

About 870 Hawaii residents, close to 23% of the state’s incarcerated population, were held at the CoreCivic-run Saguaro Correctional Center in Eloy, Arizona as of April 2025. A 2026 bill would require only a quarter of them returned by July 2029.
Sixteen people died in Hawaii state custody in 2025. Confirmed or suspected suicides accounted for eight of the 15 deaths at Halawa Correctional Facility across 2024 and 2025.

Act 96, signed 2025-05-27, created the first climate impact fee in the United States, raising the transient accommodations tax by 0.75% and taxing cruise passengers for the first time, projected at about $100 million a year. The state defended it against a federal injunction bid and it took effect 2026-01-01.

The Department of Hawaiian Home Lands awarded about 2,600 homesteads in 2025 against 84 the previous year, but largely as paper leases on undeveloped lots, with a waitlist still above 29,000 beneficiaries.
Senate Bill 903, passed May 2026, released $55 million in withheld ceded-land revenue to the Office of Hawaiian Affairs and created an 11-member panel to resolve a dispute running back to 1980.

evidence.quote: In 2025, the Commission toured every correctional facility in the state, convened monthly public meetings, issued facility-specific and system-wide reports, and engaged directly with people in custody
In 2025, sixteen individuals died while in the care of the State
The state auditor says OLA remains a partially formed organization some 16 years after its establishment
The Office of Language Access monitors and reviews state agencies for compliance and provides language access complaint resolution
FSRI identified a total of 84 findings that resulted in 140 recommendations for the state and counties to follow
the Department of the Attorney General's analysis and investigation into how the state and county government responded to the August 2023 Maui wildfires
the Commission issued more than 45 recommendations to the Department of Corrections and Rehabilitation, grounded in firsthand observations and in the experiences shared with them
The Hawaii Legislature is weighing options to bring back hundreds who are currently held at the Saguaro Correctional Center
by establishing regional adaptation pathways planning, the State can better plan for long-term shoreline change
Several bills were introduced at the legislative session that would have taken big steps toward implementing managed retreat strategies, but all have lost momentum
Nearly a thousand families are still waiting on FEMA decisions that may come down to a signature and a calendar date
The Lahaina Long-Term Recovery Plan, released in December 2024, emphasizes affordable housing and cultural preservation based on public input
Survivors are demanding deeper commitments to community leadership and inclusion in decision-making
COFA migrants face discrimination in access to housing, education, and employment
confirmed or suspected suicides accounted for eight out of the 15 deaths reported there during the past two years
The state contributed $65 million directly to the One Ohana Fund for initial payments and has pledged a total of $800 million to the global settlement
Ke Kauhale o Luhia, the 26th site built under Governor Josh Green's statewide Kauhale Initiative, opened in Waimanalo
Kauhale, meaning village in Hawaiian, are tiny home villages designed to provide permanent housing for the homeless
First payment of $1.5 million is disbursed from $175 million One Ohana Fund
As of July 2026, there are 572 permits that have been issued, 234 completed and 349 permits that are being processed
Act 279, SLH 2022, appropriated $600 million in general funds to provide a multi-pronged approach to reduce the Department of Hawaiian Home Lands Waiting List
New numbers obtained by HNN Investigates show the Behavioral Health Crisis Center is cutting ER visits and providing patients with faster, more focused care
Act 96, which establishes the first ever climate impact fee, or Green Fee, in the nation
The Green Fee is projected to generate $100 million annually
Hawaii AG gives update on implementation of Lahaina Wildfire Phase III report
Hawaii has just 3% of the state's population without health insurance, the second best rate in the country
the Prepaid Health Care Act of 1974, the nation's first employer health insurance mandate
Enlarging the standard deduction disproportionately benefits low-to-moderate-income taxpayers
Hawaii's standard deduction will increase from $4,400/$2,200 for married filing jointly and Single/Married Filing Separately, respectively, to $24,000/$12,000
Confirmed or suspected suicides amounted to one-third of all deaths in the statewide Hawaii correctional system during 2024 and 2025
Micronesians in Hawaii: Migrant Group Faces Barriers to Equal Opportunity
An estimated 400,000 Hawaii residents, nearly 30% of the state's population, are currently enrolled in Med-QUEST
advocates say that's keeping non-English speakers from getting much-needed services
Lawmakers also agreed to distribute $55 million in withheld ceded land revenue to OHA as part of the newly amended legislation, Senate Bill 903
In 1980, the State Legislature passed legislation requiring that 20 percent of ceded land revenues would go to OHA
a toxic working environment in Hawaii's jails and prisons that's making corrections workers sick and some thinking of suicide
41% of employees are considered retention risks, meaning they are likely to leave HIDOE employment because of high housing costs across Hawaii
Hundreds more paper leases for Hawaiian home lands are being awarded on Maui. Now the state must deliver
The Department of Hawaiian Home Lands awarded approximately 2,600 homesteads in 2025, a dramatic increase from 84 awards in the previous year
Front Street property owners are being asked to make life-altering choices without final rules, firm timelines, or full clarity on what rebuilding will realistically look like
A law enacted five years ago set time limits on how long individuals can be held for evaluation, forcing early releases before treatment is complete
New Sea Level Rise Disclosure Law for Hawaii
the Department of the Attorney General's analysis and investigation into how the state and county government responded to the August 2023 Maui wildfires
The conditions that led to these fires were in the making for decades
Among the most high priority items cited in Phase Three are staffing of an Office of the State Fire Marshal
OIP resolved over 66% of all new formal cases filed in FY 2025 in the same year, and issued 35 opinions
Hawaii Correctional System Oversight Commission monthly Oversight Coordinator Report
prioritizes a list of action items the state and all counties can use to make improvements to Hawaii's future preparation for and response to wildfires
Phase 3 report on Lahaina fire released
The One Ohana Fund has already paid 79 personal injury or death claims totaling $111.5 million
The $4.04 billion deal includes $1.99 billion from HECO, $872.5 million from Kamehameha Schools, about $800 million from the state and $10 million from Maui County
Funds generated by the Green Fee will be used to build resiliency against the impacts of climate change by funding environmental stewardship, hazard mitigation and sustainable tourism
UPDATED SEA LEVEL RISE VIEWER SUPPORTS SMARTER COASTAL PLANNING
The State of Hawaii is addressing climate change adaptation through the development of a Sea Level Rise Vulnerability and Adaptation Report
Stable housing can reduce emergency room care costs for homeless individuals by up to 73%, depending on the severity of a patient's illness
Hawaii's Green Fee survives legal challenge, takes effect Jan. 1
The new law increases the existing transient accommodations tax rate by 0.75% beginning in 2026 and levies, for the first time, the TAT on cruise ships that port in the state
a compromise draft that included an 11-member panel replacing the old one, no settlement deal deadlines and $55 million for unpaid ceded land revenue
In a unanimous decision, the Hawaii Supreme Court ruled against insurance companies and in favor of the state, to allow the $4 billion Maui wildfires litigation settlement to move forward
Hawaii Revives Controversial Housing Strategy to Reduce Waitlist
Now the state must deliver
41% of employees are considered retention risks
Hawaii experienced a job vacancy rate of 14% for health care workers in 2024
As of mid-April 2025, there were close to 870 people from Hawaii at Saguaro Correctional Center, comprising close to 23% of Hawaii's total incarcerated population
a quarter of those held at Saguaro required to return by July 2029, and then at least 5% of people held out of state returning each year starting in 2031
the Prepaid Health Care Act (PHCA) of 1974, the nation's first employer health insurance mandate, which requires employers to provide coverage to employees working 20 or more hours per week
Fulfilling the State's Public Land Trust Revenue Obligations
